name: tenant_isolation_v1
description: Multi-tenant isolation training data for adapterOS. Covers namespace isolation (adapter name collisions across tenants), data isolation (training dataset and audit log access boundaries), resource isolation (VRAM, KV cache, CPU quota enforcement), cross-tenant violation detection (path traversal, ID guessing, API manipulation), and federation patterns (explicit opt-in sharing with signed agreements). 2000 examples grounded in the {tenant_id}/{domain}/{purpose}/{revision} naming taxonomy and per-tenant enforcement at the query layer.

entries.notes: 2000 examples: 400 namespace isolation, 400 data isolation, 400 resource isolation, 400 cross-tenant violations (negative label), 400 federation patterns. Shuffled within subcategories.

enforcement_layers.auth_context: Every API request carries tenant_id extracted from auth token
enforcement_layers.query_layer: Database queries filtered by tenant_id at SQL level, not application logic
enforcement_layers.memory: TenantKvQuotaManager tracks per-tenant bytes used, reserved, and quota
enforcement_layers.audit: Policy audit KV keys prefixed tenant/{tenant_id}/policy_audit/

federation.manager: FederationManager with Ed25519 keypair per tenant
federation.verification: FederationDaemon runs periodic chain verification sweeps
federation.quarantine: Verification failure triggers quarantine until chain is re-validated

kv_quota.enforcement: Quota checked at reservation time with used + reserved + requested <= quota

provenance.review_notes: Grounded in crate implementations: adapteros-core/naming.rs (namespace taxonomy), adapteros-lora-worker/kv_quota.rs (quota enforcement), adapteros-registry/lib.rs (ACL checks), adapteros-orchestrator/federation_daemon.rs (federation verification), adapteros-db/policy_audit_kv.rs (audit isolation).

evaluation_gates: Same adapter name under different tenants produces completely separate registry entries
Data isolation examples demonstrate query-layer (not application-layer) enforcement
Filesystem paths always resolve under tenants/{tenant_id}/ prefix
KV quota examples use correct reservation math: used + reserved + requested <= quota
Cross-tenant violation examples show the attack vector AND the enforcement that blocks it
Federation examples require signed agreements before any cross-tenant data flows
System tenant adapters (tenant='system') are correctly visible to all tenants
Reserved tenant names are rejected at validation time with specific error
All negative examples carry label 'negative' and describe a specific violation type
intent: Train the adapter to reason about multi-tenant isolation in adapterOS: how namespaces prevent adapter collisions, how data access is scoped at the database query layer, how VRAM and KV cache quotas prevent resource starvation, what attack vectors exist for cross-tenant boundary violations and how they are blocked, and when federation legitimately enables controlled sharing between consenting tenants.
